_meta.selectionRule: Prioritize all Medical Terminology + Dosage Math + Anatomy & Physiology topics (in syllabus order), then fill to 60 with remaining blueprint topics in recommendedSequenceOrder
_meta.questionRule: Even lesson index (0-based): 3 questions; odd index: 2 questions → 150 total

lessons.title: Word Roots, Prefixes, and Suffixes
lessons.summary: Beginner-friendly introduction to Word Roots, Prefixes, and Suffixes (Medical Terminology). You will build vocabulary, core concepts, and simple practice habits that make anatomy, physiology, dosage math, and terminology stick. Pace: short study blocks, frequent self-checks, and lots of plain-language explanations—designed for pre-nursing and early nursing school readiness.
learningObjectives: Define key terms and structures relevant to Word Roots, Prefixes, and Suffixes using correct word parts and directional language.
Explain one or two “why it matters” links between this topic and safe, basic patient care reasoning.
Solve or identify beginner-level practice items (including recall-plus-light application) with attention to common mistakes.
Locate trusted next steps for study (textbook sections, lab manuals, instructor resources) when you need deeper detail.

sections.heading: What you are learning (and why first)
sections.body: Word Roots, Prefixes, and Suffixes is part of Medical Terminology. Readiness level: critical. Build word parts first—this is the fastest lever for every later science topic. If you feel lost, return to simpler vocabulary or number skills—almost every “hard” nursing topic is built from these layers.

sections.heading: Core concepts in plain language
sections.body: Work through the ideas slowly: name the parts, describe what they do, then add one layer of “what changes when…” stories. For A&P, sketch simple diagrams in your own words. For terminology, practice building and deconstructing words. For dosage math, write units every step until the habit is automatic.

sections.heading: Guided practice pattern
sections.body: Use a three-step loop: (1) recall without notes, (2) redo missed items with a short explanation, (3) teach the idea aloud in 30 seconds. This is more effective than long passive reading. Track mistakes by type (vocabulary vs calculation vs concept) so your next session targets the real weak spot.

sections.heading: Common beginner traps
sections.body: Watch for: mixing similar terms, skipping units in math, memorizing definitions without examples, and studying only right before exams. Nursing school rewards steady habits—start small, repeat often, and celebrate incremental wins.

examTips: Translate new words: find a root/prefix/suffix whenever possible.
For math, estimate first—if the magnitude is impossible, recheck before changing answers.
For A&P, ask “what happens if this fails?” to turn anatomy into physiology intuition.
Sequence hint: blueprint order #1 — keep following the path when you plan weekly study.

lessons.title: Introduction to Pharmacology: Drug Names and Classifications
lessons.subdomain: Introduction to Pharmacology: Drug Names and Classifications
lessons.summary: Beginner-friendly introduction to Introduction to Pharmacology: Drug Names and Classifications (Pharmacology Foundations). You will build vocabulary, core concepts, and simple practice habits that make anatomy, physiology, dosage math, and terminology stick. Pace: short study blocks, frequent self-checks, and lots of plain-language explanations—designed for pre-nursing and early nursing school readiness.
learningObjectives: Define key terms and structures relevant to Introduction to Pharmacology: Drug Names and Classifications using correct scientific language.
Explain one or two “why it matters” links between this topic and safe, basic patient care reasoning.
Solve or identify beginner-level practice items (including recall-plus-light application) with attention to common mistakes.
Locate trusted next steps for study (textbook sections, lab manuals, instructor resources) when you need deeper detail.

sections.heading: What you are learning (and why first)
sections.body: Introduction to Pharmacology: Drug Names and Classifications is part of Pharmacology Foundations. Readiness level: high. Keep explanations concrete with everyday comparisons; pre-nursing success is about sturdy basics, not clinical speed. If you feel lost, return to simpler vocabulary or number skills—almost every “hard” nursing topic is built from these layers.

sections.heading: Core concepts in plain language
sections.body: Work through the ideas slowly: name the parts, describe what they do, then add one layer of “what changes when…” stories. For A&P, sketch simple diagrams in your own words. For terminology, practice building and deconstructing words. For dosage math, write units every step until the habit is automatic.

sections.heading: Guided practice pattern
sections.body: Use a three-step loop: (1) recall without notes, (2) redo missed items with a short explanation, (3) teach the idea aloud in 30 seconds. This is more effective than long passive reading. Track mistakes by type (vocabulary vs calculation vs concept) so your next session targets the real weak spot.

sections.heading: Common beginner traps
sections.body: Watch for: mixing similar terms, skipping units in math, memorizing definitions without examples, and studying only right before exams. Nursing school rewards steady habits—start small, repeat often, and celebrate incremental wins.

examTips: Translate new words: find a root/prefix/suffix whenever possible.
For math, estimate first—if the magnitude is impossible, recheck before changing answers.
For A&P, ask “what happens if this fails?” to turn anatomy into physiology intuition.
Sequence hint: blueprint order #1 — keep following the path when you plan weekly study.

questions.stem: (pre-nursing; Medical Terminology; word-roots-prefixes-and-suffixes; Q1) In the term "tachycardia," which word part most specifically relates to speed or rate?
choices: A. Tachy-
B. -cardia
C. -ia
D. The root has no rate-related part
questions.rationale: "Tachy-" indicates fast or rapid; "-cardia" refers to the heart. Together they mean rapid heart rate—classic prefix + root/combining form construction.

incorrectRationales.B: -cardia references the heart itself, not the speed descriptor.
incorrectRationales.C: -ia is a noun ending; it does not carry the meaning of fast.
incorrectRationales.D: The prefix clearly signals rate in this construction.

questions.stem: (pre-nursing; Dosage Math; basic-math-fractions-decimals-percentages; Q16) A provider orders 4 mg PO once. Tablets on hand are 3 mg each. How many tablets should the student nurse calculate for one dose?
choices: A. 1.333 tablet(s)
B. 12 tablet(s)
C. 0.75 tablet(s)
D. 4000 tablet(s)
questions.rationale: Use dimensional analysis: 4 mg ÷ 3 mg/tablet = 1.33 tablet(s). Beginner-safe rounding rules follow your program policy; here the math points to the fractional tablet answer as the calculated quantity.

incorrectRationales.B: Multiplying mg by tablet strength does not yield a dose count; this confuses dose with stock strength.
incorrectRationales.C: Inverting the division gives a nonsensical fraction for most real tablet sizes.
incorrectRationales.D: Micrograms are not requested; unit mismatch is a common dosage error on exams.

questions.stem: (pre-nursing; Anatomy & Physiology; organization-of-the-human-body; Q31) Which statement is the most accurate beginner-level description of homeostasis?
choices: A. The body maintains stable internal conditions through feedback mechanisms
B. Homeostasis means all organs work at the same speed at all times
C. Homeostasis only applies during sleep
D. Homeostasis prevents the body from ever changing a lab value
questions.rationale: Homeostasis describes regulated stability (e.g., temperature, blood pressure) using feedback loops—not identical organ speeds, sleep-only processes, or frozen lab values.

incorrectRationales.B: Organs vary activity by need; homeostasis is about regulated balance, not uniform speed.
incorrectRationales.C: Homeostasis operates continuously, not only during sleep.
incorrectRationales.D: Healthy physiology allows values to change within regulated ranges.
